title: PTPN11 and RAS pathway mutations in AML
learning_objectives: Distinguish diagnostic, prognostic and clonal markers
clinical_vignette: A 55-year-old man has AML with NPM1 mutation. NGS also detects PTPN11 mutation and NRAS mutation. FLT3-ITD is negative.
question: How should PTPN11/NRAS mutations be interpreted?
options: A. They are cooperating signalling pathway mutations and should be integrated with the main AML-defining lesion.
B. They define acute promyelocytic leukaemia.
correct_answer: A. They are cooperating signalling pathway mutations and should be integrated with the main AML-defining lesion.
explanation: PTPN11 and RAS pathway mutations are recurrent cooperating lesions in AML. They may affect biology and prognosis but usually do not define the diagnostic AML entity alone.
frcpath_pearl: Classify the AML first, then interpret cooperating signalling mutations.
take_home_message: Molecular findings in AML must be interpreted in diagnostic hierarchy and clinical context.
references: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours, 5th Edition
